The increasing order of diazotisation of the following compounds is: (a)
(b)
(c)
(d)
question:
The increasing order of diazotisation of the following compounds is: (a)
(b)
(c)
(d)
solution: Aromatic diazonium salts are more stable than aliphatic diazonium salts. The higher stability of aryl diazonium salts is due to resonance. Electron donating substituents increase electron density on benzene ring. Hereby they increase the stability of diazonium salts. Electron withdrawing substituents decrease electron density on benzene ring. Hereby they decrease the stability of diazonium salts. $-\mathrm{COCH}_3$ group is electron withdrawing and hence, diazonium salts from (D) is less stable than that from (B). Although-O- $\mathrm{COCH}_3$ is electron donating substituent, but it is present in meta position. Hence, it will not have significant effect on stability. The increasing order of diazotisation is (A) $ < $ (D) $ < $ (B) $ < $ (C).

q: The mass of benzanilide obtained from the benzoylation reaction of 5.8 g of aniline, if yield of product is $82 \%$, is $\_\_\_\_$ g (nearest integer). (Given molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{H}: 1, \mathrm{C}: 12, \mathrm{~N}: 14, \mathrm{O}: 16$)
question: The mass of benzanilide obtained from the benzoylation reaction of 5.8 g of aniline, if yield of product is $82 \%$, is $\_\_\_\_$ g (nearest integer). (Given molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{H}: 1, \mathrm{C}: 12, \mathrm{~N}: 14, \mathrm{O}: 16$)
solution:
Benzoylation of aniline:
$C_6H_5NH_2 + C_6H_5COCl \rightarrow C_6H_5CONHC_6H_5 + HCl$

Molar mass of aniline:
$M = 6(12) + 7(1) + 14 = 93$ g/mol

Moles of aniline:
$n = \frac{5.8}{93} = 0.0624$ mol

Molar mass of benzanilide ($C_{13}H_{11}NO$):
$M = 13(12) + 11(1) + 14 + 16 = 197$ g/mol

Theoretical mass of benzanilide:
$m_{theoretical} = 0.0624 \times 197 = 12.29$ g

Actual mass with 82% yield:
$m_{actual} = 12.29 \times 0.82 = 10.08 \approx 10$ g

q: The increasing basicity order of the following compounds is:
question: The increasing basicity order of the following compounds is:
solution:
In case of polar protic solvents, two factors decide the basic character. The positive inductive effect from alkyl groups which increases basicity.

3o>2o>1o Amine

& the Steric Hindrance. 1o>2 >3o  

Thus, the resultant basic character is determined by taking the above two factors into account.

So order will be 2o>1o>3o if methyl group is present as alkyl group otherwise order will be 2°>3°>1°.

In aromatic & Aliphatic: Aliphatic Amine are more basic than aromatic because the lone pair of N is involved in delocalization.

So basic character is in the order 2o>1o>3o>Aromatic Amine

So (B) > (A) > (C) > (D)

subject: Chemistry
q: The Hinsberg reagent is
question: The Hinsberg reagent is
solution:
Benzenesulphonyl chloride C6H5SO2Cl, which is also known as Hinsberg's reagent, reacts with primary and secondary amines to form sulphonamides.

The Hinsberg test is based on the production of sulfonamides. An amine interacts with benzene sulfonyl chloride in the Hinsberg test. Due to the inability of tertiary amines to form stable sulfonamides, any product produced is either a primary or secondary amine. If the resulting sulfonamide dissolves in aqueous sodium hydroxide solution, it is a primary amine. It is a secondary amine if the sulfonamide is insoluble in aqueous sodium hydroxide and hydrogen chloride.

q: A compound with molecular mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn></math> is acylated with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COCl</mi></math> to get a compound with molecular mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>390</mn></math>. The number of amino groups presents per molecule of the former compound is
question: A compound with molecular mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn></math> is acylated with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COCl</mi></math> to get a compound with molecular mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>390</mn></math>. The number of amino groups presents per molecule of the former compound is
solution:
After acylation the formula will be R-(NHCOCH3)x. Hence, the change in the molecular mass is calculated as below

For 1one NH2→14+2=16 unitsWhen It turns into NH−COCH3  ​  →14+1+12+16+12+3=58 Change in mass⇒58−16=42units. So for every NH2 , there will be an increase of 42g after acylation.  ⇒ The total increase in mass = 390−180 = 210  ⇒ No. of amino groups=21042= 5 Hence number of amino groups are 5.

(12+16+12+3-1)x=390-180

or 42x=210; or x=5

subject: Chemistry
q: ' A ' is a neutral organic compound ($\mathrm{M}. \mathrm{F}: \mathrm{C}_{8} \mathrm{H}_{9} \mathrm{ON}$). On treatment with aqueous $\mathrm{Br}_{2} / \mathrm{HO}^{(-)}$, ' A ' forms a compound ' B ' which is soluble in dilute acid. ' B ' on treatment with aqueous $\mathrm{NaNO}_{2} / \mathrm{HCl}\left(0-5^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ produces a compound ' C ' which on treatment with $\mathrm{CuCN} / \mathrm{NaCN}$ produces ' D '. Hydrolysis of ' D ' produces ' E ' which is also obtainable from the hydrolysis of ' A '. ' E ' on treatment with acidified $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ produces ' $F$ '. ' $F$ ' contains two different types of hydrogen atoms. The structure of ' A ' is
question: ' A ' is a neutral organic compound ($\mathrm{M}. \mathrm{F}: \mathrm{C}_{8} \mathrm{H}_{9} \mathrm{ON}$). On treatment with aqueous $\mathrm{Br}_{2} / \mathrm{HO}^{(-)}$, ' A ' forms a compound ' B ' which is soluble in dilute acid. ' B ' on treatment with aqueous $\mathrm{NaNO}_{2} / \mathrm{HCl}\left(0-5^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ produces a compound ' C ' which on treatment with $\mathrm{CuCN} / \mathrm{NaCN}$ produces ' D '. Hydrolysis of ' D ' produces ' E ' which is also obtainable from the hydrolysis of ' A '. ' E ' on treatment with acidified $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ produces ' $F$ '. ' $F$ ' contains two different types of hydrogen atoms. The structure of ' A ' is
solution:
Compound A undergoes bromination with aqueous Br₂/HO⁻ to form B, which is soluble in dilute acid, indicating an amino group is present.
B undergoes diazotization with NaNO₂/HCl at 0-5°C to form diazonium compound C.
The diazonium compound is then treated with CaCN/NaOH to form nitrile compound D.
Hydrolysis of D produces carboxylic acid E.
When A is treated with acidified KMnO₄, it produces F containing two different types of hydrogen atoms.

This reaction sequence is characteristic of anthranilic acid (2-aminobenzoic acid), where the amino group directs bromination to the para position, and subsequent reactions proceed through the diazonium intermediate.

The structure shown in option (3), which is 2-aminobenzoic acid, fits all these transformations.

solution:
Diazotization: The process of conversion of a primary aromatic amino compound into a diazonium salt is known as diazotization. This process is carried out by adding an aqueous solution of sodium nitrite to a solution of primary aromatic amine (e.g., aniline) in excess of HCl at a temperature below 5°C.

Decomposition of benzene diazonium chloride by using Cu2Cl2/HCl to form chlorobenzene is known as sandmeyer's reaction.

q: The final product A, formed in the following multistep reaction sequence is:
question: The final product A, formed in the following multistep reaction sequence is:
solution:
Bromobenzene reacts with magnesium in dry ether (diethyl ether) to form phenylmagnesium bromide, often abbreviated as PhMgBr.

The resulting phenylmagnesium bromide reacts with carbon dioxide in the presence of an acidic medium to form benzoic acid.

Heating benzoic acid with ammonia can lead to a reaction known as the Schotten-Baumann type reaction, which is a method for the amidation of carboxylic acids. In this reaction, the carboxylic acid group of benzoic acid reacts with ammonia  to form an amide, benzamide, under heating conditions.

When an amide is treated with bromine in an aqueous or ethanolic solution of sodium hydroxide, degradation of amide takes place leading to the formation of primary amine.

In this case, it is an hoffmann bromamide reaction, aniline is formed.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Total number of alkali insoluble solid sulphonamides obtained by reaction of given amines with Hinsberg's reagent is $\_\_\_\_$.
Aniline, N-Methylaniline, Methanamine, N, N-Dimethylmethanamine, N-Methyl methanamine, Phenylmethanamine, N-propylaniline, N-phenylaniline, N, N-Dimethylaniline, Allyl amine, Isopropyl amine
question:
Total number of alkali insoluble solid sulphonamides obtained by reaction of given amines with Hinsberg's reagent is $\_\_\_\_$.
Aniline, N-Methylaniline, Methanamine, N, N-Dimethylmethanamine, N-Methyl methanamine, Phenylmethanamine, N-propylaniline, N-phenylaniline, N, N-Dimethylaniline, Allyl amine, Isopropyl amine
solution:
Hinsberg's reagent (benzenesulfonyl chloride) classifies amines by their reaction products:
Primary amines (RNH₂) form alkali-soluble sulfonamides (soluble in NaOH).
Secondary amines (R₂NH) form alkali-insoluble sulfonamides (precipitate in NaOH).
Tertiary amines (R₃N) do not react.
Analyzing the given amines:
Aniline (primary), Methanamine (primary), Phenylmethanamine (primary), Allyl amine (primary), Isopropyl amine (primary) → alkali-soluble.
N,N-Dimethylmethanamine (tertiary), N,N-Dimethylaniline (tertiary) → no reaction.
N-Methylaniline (secondary), N-Methyl methanamine (secondary), N-Propylaniline (secondary), N-Phenylaniline (secondary) → alkali-insoluble.
Total alkali-insoluble sulfonamides: 4.
